speaker: interviewer
text: 201 With Victor Ungar.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today, February 15th, 1995. The survivor's name is Victor Ungar. The interviewer, Sandy Jacobson. The city and state, Los Angeles, California. Country, United States. And the language will be English. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Ungar, thank you very much for participating in the project. Please tell me your full name and when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name is Victor Ungar, U-N-G-A-R. I was born in Kosice-- it's a city in Eastern Slovakia-- on September 26, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in Kosice. My father owned a bank. My mother had her own business. She had a textile business. And my father was also the pres-- the President of the Orthodox Jewish congregation. I had two brothers and a sister. One brother survived. My older sister and my younger sis-- brother were taken to concentration camp with my mother, and they were-- they never came back. My father joined me in Slovakia later on, and he was taken to Auschwitz in 1944, Sept-- September-- by the end of September. I-- I went to school in my hometown. It was a state-run school, but it was in the building of the Jewish community. Half a day, I went to-- to secular classes, and In the afternoon till the evening, I went to yeshiva. Later on, after I graduated and I couldn't go to any higher schools because I was Jewish, I-- I-- and I couldn't get an appointment, it was impossible, and so I went to the Jewish yeshiva all day until I left my hometown, and went to Slovakia for a couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back and talk a little bit about your home town. Was it-- how many people lived here?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, the city had about 75,000 inhabitants. Out of the 75,000 inhabitants, about 10,000 were Jewish. Probably 5,000 were Orthodox, and the other 5,000 were reformed or conservative.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father was the president of a community, the orthodox community. How did that operate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had a rabbi. They had two big temples. Then, they had several other temples that were ultra-orthodox. And they had-- they had several rabbis, in fact. And-- and they had religious schools. They operated religious groups too. And they had an organization that took care of the people who couldn't afford-- who-- of the poor people. And I guess that was the extent of the operation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father's position-- was that an elected or appointed position?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an elected position, and without remuneration. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was the last one, the last Jewish president before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did he serve in that position?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe he was president the last five years. Before that, he was a controller and vice president.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your home life a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we were orthodox. When I say orthodox, my father didn't have a beard or didn't-- wasn't one of those ultra-orthodox people. He-- he was in a-- he had a bank, so he had to look sort of like all the other people look. And my mother had her own business when I was little. When we grew up, we had a maid who took care of us, and we had a Jewish person who took care of the household, so just to be sure that the we had kosher-- that the household was a kosher household.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do in the textile business?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was selling-- it was a small store, and she was selling all kinds of textile goods for people who-- who bought them, and had them done, sa-- custom made.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- was it unusual for a woman to work in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was unusual. Usually, the women stayed home. But she preferred to be in business. So she had her own store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else in your house with you? Grandparents? Aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but there was always an aunt, or an uncle, or cousin who lived with us for a short while. I had uncle-- an uncle who studied in a yeshiva in Galanta. That was a Slovakian town in western Slovakia. And after he finished his studies, he came and lived with us for a couple of years. I also had-- my aunt-- there was always one of my father's sisters or brothers who lived with us on and off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- can you describe your house? Do you still remember what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My house, yeah, we had-- we had, I believe, three bedrooms, we had a dining room, and we had a living room, and a kitchen, and a maid's quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We lived in a five-unit apartment building. So there was a garden there, but it was-- it was just a communal garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about this school that you went to. You said that it was a state school that was located in the Jewish community building. What was the relationship between the state, between the city or local government, and the Jews of Kosice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was pretty-- pretty good. And I think this was an accommodation for the Jewish-- Jewish community. They couldn't afford to run a school, a private school, so the state-- and in order for to accommodate the Jewish students on Jewish holidays and on the Sabbath, they accommodated us, and-- and sort of leased the facilities, and had their teachers teach the kids who wouldn't go on the Sabbath to school. That was only the first five years. Later on, after the fifth grade, we had to attend a school that was run-- that was in a-- in a-- in a state-run-- state building. But they grouped us in a Jewish class, so we, on the Sabbath, when we had to attend school, we didn't have to write. The subjects were such that didn't need any-- writing. And only-- there were only subjects that the teacher was-- was either lecturing or examining, orally, us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it common for children to go to school on Sabbath? On Saturdays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was-- it was a common thing to go. They had schools-- the non-Jewish kids, or the kids who were not observant, they had to go on Saturdays to school too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went six days a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six days a week, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were-- how were the relations between the Jews and the non-Jews in your town? Let's start out talking about as kids. Was there anti-Semitism as a kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was anti-Semitism. Many times, after we got through our yeshiva, we were met sometimes with four or five non-Jewish kids who threw rocks at us, and sometimes even injured some of the kids who came out of that school. But it was during the Czechoslovakian regime, we always got police protection. We had very good relations with the police. In fact, my father was always on very good terms with-- with the local government. Whenever the mayor was running for office, he'd always seek the votes of the Jewish people, and came to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on a Sabbath afternoon, usually came to a meeting, and wanted to-- wanted to raise the issues, and was so-- and soliciting the votes of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, one of the-- one of the priests, Catholic priests, was running for a state office, not a city office. On a Saturday afternoon, my father took me to that meeting. He even quoted from the [NON-ENGLISH]. [HEBREW]. If I don't do things for myself, who will do it? So I remember he made a point to say something in Hebrew, just to endear himself in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the voters. So-- so, the Jewish people in that country, in eastern Slovakia, had full rights of ownership and voting, et cetera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up until 1938. After 1938, when-- after Chamberlain struck a deal with Hitler and part of Czechoslovakia was ceded to Germany, and my city was ceded to Hungary, at that point in time, it started to deteriorate. Our position started to deteriorate. My-- my-- the license for my bank-- for my father's bank was revoked. The license for my mother's store was revoked also. We-- she still had a store, but in partnership with another Jewish person whose-- whose license was not revoked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about the war years, let's go back again to go to your life as a child. Can you remember how you would celebrate Jewish holidays? Were there dinners, et cetera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, we were orthodox. Sabbaths were strictly observed. We had a Fri-- every Friday night a Friday night dinner-- traditional dinner with singing, and with whatever the Sabbath dinner had to have. And we went to the temple before the dinner. And next day, we went-- in the morning, we went to-- we went to the temple again. And after we came home, we had lunch. And in the afternoons, again, we used-- we went to our yeshiva every Saturday afternoon. And in the evening, we came back, and we waited till the end of the Sabbath. And then-- it was a traditional Sabbath in our house. Sundays we-- we also went to yeshiva. We never had-- there was no such a thing as a day off. And Saturdays, in the mornings, when I-- after my fifth grade, I had to attend school. In the morning, I had to go to temple early in the morning in order to-- for the services.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandparents live in the same town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My grandfather-- my paternal grandfather-- lived in Bratislava. That was the capital of Slovakia, in western Slovakia, just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 20 or 40 miles from Vienna. And that was my father's hometown. And my paternal-- my maternal grandparents, they were much older than my paternal grandparents. They lived in a small village by the name of Medzilaborce. That was northeastern Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go visit them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I visited my-- my maternal grandmother once, I believe. My grandmother visited with us several times. Came to-- I believe she was seeking the medical attention in our town. And my paternal grandfather and grandmother, I also visited just once, in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, let's go to the point-- in 1938, you would've been 14 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How aware were you of the-- what was happening in Europe, and how that would affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1938, was the-- that was the year when-- when our city was ceded to Hungary, and things started to be hard. The-- they had revoked the licenses of my parents, and we just hoped that things would get better. And we tried to get away from Slovakia, from my hometown. But there was no place to go. There was-- there was a plan to go to Kenya, but it fizzled out. The whole-- some part of our community people wanted to go there, but it just fizzled out. It just wasn't the place to go to, and I guess we couldn't even have gone there, for some other reasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was your father able to keep working in his bank?

speaker: interviewee
text: 193-- not after 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about his position as president of the Orthodox Community?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was president all the way to the end.

words.text: all the way to the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did he, in that position, have-- did he try to deal with the new authorities?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was dealing, and-- and he-- he didn't have a hard time. He was friendly with the police. He was friendly with the civil government. In fact, he was so friendly that, before we were deported, he made the-- the president of the local [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] county government promise that, if he-- if we are deported, that he'll hold our apartment. He won't give it away. He'll keep it, so when we come back, we should get it back. And he kept his promise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew you were going to be deported out of town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea when or where you would be taken? Let's start, did you have any idea when it would happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- up until April-- April of 1944, we-- we lived in peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your own town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Relatively in peace. We had some infringements of rights, but we-- we were allowed to vote. We were still voting. And we had full-- sort of full citizenship rights. There was some-- Jewish kids couldn't go to higher high schools from four-- from eighth grade up. There was [NON-ENGLISH]. And to-- to universities, there was no way to get into.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you go to school, then, after the eighth grade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just went to our Jewish schools. Since I didn't have any other way of employing myself, you know, just we-- just continued going to school to study at the yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, as you pointed out, from '38 to '44, did not work in the bank at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He sort of kept busy with communal affairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive financially?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was very well-off, so we didn't have a problem. And my mother still had her textile business, and-- on-- in the partnership, sort of. The other people-- we supplied the financial part, and the other people fin-- supplied the-- the license. So she was able to continue her business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So actually, did you have any idea of what was happening in other parts of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that just 20 or 30 mile away, across the Hungarian-Slovakian border, in 1942, my paternal grandparents and their children were deported. We didn't know where. And we were-- we were aware that they might not-- they might never come back. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's brother, who was also a communal leader in Bratislava, he wrote us. He sent us the last postcard that my grandfather asked him to write. He wrote it out himself, and the r-- usually, when my grandfather wrote a note, or a letter, or a postcard, he usually sent us-- once a week, we received a card from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on top of the card, there was always [NON-ENGLISH]. The-- are you familiar with it? Usually, the religious people, when they write letters, on top of the letters, whether it's written in English or any other language, they start out with the letter [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], that means-- means 'the help of God.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And after addressing your-- the person whom you wrote the letter, you-- they noted with an [NON-ENGLISH] and [INAUDIBLE], that you should live till 120 years. And he omitted those two Hebrew notations, so he knew that-- the state of mind he was in, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but there was nothing we could do. They never-- some of those people tried to come over to Hungary illegally, but I guess they were caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean to come from Hungary to Slovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Slovakia. From Slovakia to Hungary at that time. So most of the Jews were, in 1942, deported to-- not to Auschwitz-- or some were to Auschwitz. But I believe they were taken to Kamianets-Podilskyi. It's a Polish town, and I guess they were gunned down. Maybe they were-- I believe they were made to dig their own graves, and they were gunned down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know which group your grandparents were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never found out. But they went with their children. And-- we did not-- there were rumors, but we didn't believe it. We thought, it's only rumors. And after 1942, there were rumors that Germans are gassing people away, but we just didn't believe it, that-- that the Germans would do such a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you continued, then, to live in your town until 1944?

speaker: interviewer
text: When in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe in March. The Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In March, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the Slo-- when the Russian front was-- when the Russians were starting to beat the Germans, and the Germans were retreating to the south, when they came to the Carpathian Mountains-- that was the common Hungarian and Polish frontier border at that point in time-- and they stopped them at that-- the Germans stopped the Russian advance at that point. The Germans, and I believe the Hungarians-- they were afraid, the Germans, that the Hungarians will switch sides. The-- Hungary was occupied by Germany. I believe in the morning, we woke up, and the radio announced that the German troops came in to help defend Hungary. And next day, the Germans authorities, through the Hungarian police, notified the leadership-- the Jewish leadership-- that they want everyone to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to write-- you know, they had to come to the-- they had to write a ros-- a rostrum of-- of Jewish inhabitants. And-- and they also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] required that the Jewish community-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a Jewish council was-- that they form a joint Jewish council, meaning the conservative and the orthodox had to get together and form a Jewish council. And they demanded workers, the Germans. And they also demanded that the Jewish community issue bulletins written-- to the Jewish people. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's when it started. That's-- at that point in time, you know, I had to go, many times, to work. I was-- for the Germans, either to the Gestapo headquarters to clean the place for them. And some of the Jewish-- best Jewish homes were taken by the Gestapo officers. And we knew that it's not going to get any better. But we thought that maybe, since the Russians are that close, that hopefully-- they were maybe 50 miles from our home town-- that they'll break through the front and-- and it will be a temporary-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] temporary inconvenience. But it turned out to be that they were holding the front there, the Germans. And-- and so, since Slovakia, just across the border, was in a very-- was almost Judenreit-- rein, meaning not too many Jews left there, the only Jews that stayed in Slovakia after '44 were the ones that they felt they needed for their economic health. There were doctors who were allowed to stay alive, stay with their families. And some people who owned businesses, they felt that they couldn't run without the Jews. The businesses were taken away, but they were allowed to run and get a salary to-- to run the business.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother had a brother, in the city of village of [? Michalovce, ?] who owned a business and was exempt from the deportation. And so did my father, who had an uncle in Bratislava. So they decided, since I was-- I was 18 years old-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 18 years old already, that I better go to Slovakia, because they were taking me-- they were afraid they would take to forced-- to some labor camps. So I went over the border, and I went to-- in fact, I went to my aunt's town, who lived in Humenne. Now, let me get clear. You're-- you said you're going from the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your town had been in Slovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yea-- no, my town was in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK. So originally, it had been Slovakia. But now, it was part of Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of Hungary. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you went back into Slovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back into Slovakia, and I stayed there for a couple of weeks. And the fighting started to intensify on those-- on the border of Slovakia and-- and Poland. The Russian troops were trying to break through the mountains there, the Carpathian Mountains.
